claim: Landsat thermal-infrared data at 30-100m resolution provides land-surface temperature for intra-urban heat-island mapping since 1984, enabling neighborhood-scale identification of hotspots and green-space cooling effects.

source.title: Landsat Surface Temperature Product Guide (LSDS-1330) — USGS

method: Reviewed Landsat thermal-band documentation for spatial resolution, temporal coverage, atmospheric correction, and validation studies in urban environments.
limitations: Landsat measures land-surface temperature (LST), not air temperature — the relationship between LST and the air temperature that drives heat-stress physiology varies by climate zone, humidity, and urban geometry.
Landsat has a 16-day revisit cycle with cloud-cover gaps — capturing peak heat-wave conditions requires temporal compositing.
The 30-100m resolution may not capture micro-scale cooling effects within individual green spaces or between closely spaced buildings.
LST validation against ground air-temperature stations is sparse in LMIC cities — the LST-to-air-temperature transfer function has not been calibrated for most tropical urban contexts.

claim: The cooling effect of urban green space depends critically on irrigation — in water-scarce cities, unirrigated vegetation provides minimal cooling during heat waves when cooling is most needed, creating a fundamental constraint for green-space cooling in many LMIC cities.

source.title: Irrigation dependence of urban green space cooling under heat waves

method: Reviewed irrigation-cooling relationship studies, water-scarcity assessments for LMIC cities, and evapotranspiration modeling under heat-wave conditions.
limitations: The irrigation-cooling relationship varies by climate zone, soil type, and vegetation species — local calibration is needed for specific cities.
Water-scarcity assessments are city-level aggregates that may not capture neighborhood-level water availability for green-space irrigation.
The finding that unirrigated vegetation provides minimal cooling during heat waves may not apply to all vegetation types — deep-rooted native species may maintain transpiration under water stress.
Irrigation requirements for green-space cooling may conflict with drinking-water and agricultural water demand in water-scarce LMIC cities.
